sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
लोकेऽस्मिन्द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ ।
ज्ञानयोगेन सांख्यानां कर्मयोगेन योगिनाम् ॥ ३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
loke 'smin dvi-vidhā niṣṭhā purā proktā mayānagha |
jñāna-yogena sāṅkhyānāṁ karma-yogena yoginām || 3 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O sinless Arjuna, I have already explained that there are two classes of men who try to realize the self. Some are inclined to understand it by empirical, philosophical speculation, and others by devotional service.

The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O sinless Arjuna, I have already explained that there are two classes of men who try to realize the self. Some are inclined to understand it by empirical, philosophical speculation, and others by devotional service.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1944 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In the Second Chapter, verse 39, the Lord explained two kinds of procedures – namely sāṅkhya-yoga and karma-yoga, or buddhi-yoga. In this verse, the Lord explains the same more clearly.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.3 Anagha, O unblemished one, O sinless one; [This word of address suggests that Arjuna is qualified to receive the Lord’s instruction.] dvividha, two kinds of ; nistha, steadfastness, persistence in what is undertaken; asmin loke, in this world, for the people of the three castes who are qualified…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-3-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading: the Teacher grants two SEPARATE niṣṭhās (against any combination-theory) — jñāna-yoga as one complete path, karma-yoga as another. The verse names karma-yoga as the questioner's path SPECIFICALLY, not because karma-yoga is universally superior, but because it is the appropriate path for someone in his state.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.3 The Lord said — You have not properly understood what I taught you before. In this world, full of people with varying degrees of qualifications, I have taught in the days of yore two ways, that of knowledge (Jnana Yoga) and that of works, according to the qualifications of aspirants.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-3-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the two niṣṭhās suit different states of inner purity. jñāna-yoga of the contemplatives suits those whose old karma has been purified and whose inner instrument is already subdued; karma-yoga of the yogis suits those whose purification proceeds through action. Both reach the same goal, but through different routes appropriate to fitness.

positions.rendering: two-fold steadfastness / niṣṭhā as standing-firm in a discipline
two-fold disciplines / fitness for two paths

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: niṣṭhā literally means 'standing firm, steadfastness' — the locked-in commitment to a particular discipline. The Advaita reading emphasizes that the verse declares two SEPARATE niṣṭhās (against any combination-theory): jñāna-yoga as one complete path, karma-yoga as another. The Vishishtadvaita reading emphasizes that the two niṣṭhās suit different temperaments / states of inner purity, but both reach the same goal. Prabhupada's 'two classes of men' captures the temperamental sorting; Mukundananda's 'two paths' captures the path-distinction. None is wrong; the disagreement is about whether the two paths are independent (Advaita) or sequenced (Vishishtadvaita: karma-yoga purifies → jñāna-yoga arises).

literal_meaning: The Lord said: O sinless one, in this world I declared previously two kinds of niṣṭhā — the jñāna-yoga of the contemplatives and the karma-yoga of the yogis.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.3 is the Teacher's first answer. He distinguishes two niṣṭhās (steadfast disciplines) declared previously: jñāna-yoga for the contemplatives (those temperamentally suited to contemplation) and karma-yoga for the yogis (those temperamentally suited to action). Per the Advaita reading the two paths are independent (rejecting the combination-theory). Per the Vishishtadvaita reading both reach the same goal but through different fitness; karma-yoga is the path being recommended TO THE QUESTIONER specifically because of his state, not because it is universally superior.
